document.title: The Prevalence of Uncommon Fractures on Skeletal Surveys Performed to Evaluate for Suspected Abuse in 930 Children: Should Practice Guidelines Change?
document.documentType: Pediatric Imaging Original Research journal article
standards: ACR practice guideline for skeletal surveys in children
ACR appropriateness criteria: suspected physical abuse—child
Standard for skeletal surveys in suspected non-accidental injury (NAI) in children

sourceSummary.oneLine: AJR original research article evaluating fracture prevalence and skeletal survey image utility in 930 children younger than 2 years with suspected abuse.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Clinical literature evidence for skeletal survey practice; the source is not presented as an FDA regulatory guidance or product-specific document.
keyPoints: The study identified skeletal surveys performed for suspected child abuse from January 1, 2003, through December 31, 2009.
The study included children younger than 2 years because ACR guidelines recommend a full skeletal survey in this age group.
Fractures were detected in 317 of 930 children, and multiple fractures were seen in 166 children.
Confirmed fractures in the spine, pelvis, hands, and feet were rare, occurring in 10 of 930 children.
The authors concluded that consideration may be given to eliminating spine, pelvis, hands, and feet views from routine skeletal surveys for suspected child abuse.

evidence.claim: The article was published in AJR in July 2011.
The article is a Pediatric Imaging Original Research publication.
evidence.evidenceText: Pe d i a t r i c I m a g i n g • O r i g i n a l R e s e a r c h

evidence.claim: The study objective was to evaluate fracture prevalence and site on skeletal surveys for suspected child abuse and whether images could be eliminated.
evidence.evidenceText: The objective of our study was to evaluate the prevalence and site of fractures detected on skeletal surveys performed for suspected child abuse at a tertiary children’s hospital and to determine whether any survey images may be eliminated without affecting clinical care or the ability to make a diagnosis.

evidence.claim: The study included skeletal surveys from 2003 through 2009 in children younger than 2 years.
evidence.evidenceText: We identified all skeletal surveys performed for suspected abuse from 2003 to 2009 of children younger than 2 years.

evidence.claim: The study included 930 children with a median age of 6 months.
evidence.evidenceText: Nine hundred thirty children (515 boys and 415 girls) with a median age of 6 months met the entry criteria for the study.

evidence.claim: Fractures were detected in 317 children, representing 34% of the study population.
The most common fracture sites were long bones, ribs, skull, and clavicle.
evidence.evidenceText: The most common sites for fractures were the long bones (21%), ribs (10%), skull (7%), and clavicle (2%).
evidence.pageTarget: Fracture distribution in suspected child abuse

evidence.claim: Confirmed fractures in the spine, pelvis, hands, and feet occurred in 10 children, representing 1% of the study population.
evidence.evidenceText: Overall, fractures in the spine, pelvis, hands, and feet were confirmed in 10 children (1%).

evidence.claim: The study reported that all 10 children with confirmed spine, pelvis, hands, or feet fractures had other signs of physical abuse.
evidence.evidenceText: All 10 children had other signs of physical abuse.

evidence.claim: The authors concluded that consideration may be given to eliminating spine, pelvis, hands, and feet views from routine skeletal surveys for suspected child abuse.
evidence.evidenceText: Given the rarity of fractures of the spine, pelvis, hands, and feet, consideration may be given to eliminating those views from routine skeletal surveys performed to evaluate for suspected child abuse.

evidence.claim: The ACR guidelines recommend a minimum skeletal survey protocol of 20 radiographs.
evidence.evidenceText: The ACR skeletal survey guidelines comprise a minimum of 20 radiographs [6].

evidence.claim: The facility's skeletal survey technique included 31 radiographs.
evidence.evidenceText: Throughout the 7-year study period, skeletal surveys performed for suspected child abuse routinely included 31 radiographs

evidence.claim: The skeletal surveys used high-resolution digital radiography equipment from Philips Healthcare or Canon.
evidence.evidenceText: All skeletal surveys were performed using high-resolution digital radiography (either Philips Healthcare DigitalDiagnost or Canon DR cassette CXDI-31).

evidence.claim: The study was approved by an institutional review board and a consent waiver was granted.
evidence.evidenceText: This study was approved by our institutional review board and a consent waiver was granted.
